1: Yādaʿtā ʿēt lèḏet yaʿălê-sālaʿ: 'Do you know when the mountain goats give birth?' — yĕʿēlê sālaʿ (mountain goats of the rock): the ibex inhabiting inaccessible cliff terrain. Yādaʿtā is the divine interrogative formula deployed through this entire speech: a repeated assault on the limits of human knowledge. The animal-gallery speech answers Job's demand for a hearing with something unexpected: not legal defense but a tour of wild creation.
2: Tispōr yĕrāḥîm temallēʾnāh: 'Can you count the months they carry young?' — sāpar (to count, number, keep record): the record-keeping that only the Creator maintains. Yāḏaʿtā ʿēt liddtān: 'do you know their time of birth?' — ʿēt (appointed time): the Creator knows the appointed time of every animal birth; Job does not.
3: Tikraʿnāh yĕladĕhennāh: 'They crouch and deliver their young' — the birthing posture (kāraʿ, to kneel). Ḥebĕlêhen yĕšallēḥnāh: 'they are released from their birth-pangs' — ḥēbel (birth-pang, cord): the labor is completed and the pain released without human assistance.
4: Yiḥlĕṣû bĕnêhem yirbû babbār: 'Their young thrive and grow in the open field' — ḥālaṣ (to be strong, thrive): independent maturation. Yāṣĕʾû wĕlōʾ-šābû lāmmô: 'they go out and do not return' — the complete independence: no mother's return, no human oversight. The wild creature is entirely outside the human economy.
5: Mî-šillaḥ pereʾ ḥopšî: 'Who set the wild donkey free?' — pereʾ (wild donkey, onager): the paradigmatic wild animal (cf. Job 6:5; Genesis 16:12). Môsĕrôt ʾărôd mî pittēaḥ: 'who opened the bonds of the onager?' — ʾărôd (onager, a species of wild ass): parallel term. The freedom of the wild ass is God's gift, not human concession.
6: ʾĂšer-śamtî ʿărābāh bêtô: 'I have given the wilderness as its home' — ʿărābāh (steppe, desert, salt flats): the uninhabitable terrain that is nevertheless the wild donkey's natural home. The Creator designated a dwelling for the wild creature in the landscape humans cannot use.
7: Yiśḥaq lĕhamôn qiryāh: 'It mocks the noise of the city' — the wild donkey's freedom manifested as contempt for human civilization and its constraints. Tĕšûʾat nôgēś lōʾ yišmāʿ: 'it does not hear the driver's shout' — nôgēś (taskmaster, driver): the creature that will not be pressed into service, unlike the domesticated donkey.
8: Yĕtûr hārîm mirʿēhû: 'The mountain range is its pasture' — tûr (to range, explore): active foraging over vast territory. Aḥar kol-yārôq yidĕrôš: 'it searches out every bit of green' — the wild creature's self-sufficient foraging, needing no human farmer to provide for it.
9: Hăyōʾbeh rĕʾēm ʿābĕḏekā: 'Will the wild ox agree to serve you?' — rĕʾēm (wild ox, aurochs): the great wild bovine, now extinct, ancestor of domesticated cattle. The LXX translates monokeros (unicorn), leading to centuries of confusion. The term refers to the mighty wild aurochs, utterly untameable. ʾAbēḏekā (your servant): the domestication-service that the wild ox refuses.
10: Hătiḵšōr rĕʾēm: 'Can you harness the wild ox?' — kāšar (to bind, harness): the harnessing of a work-animal. Yĕśaddēd ʿămāqîm ʾaḥărêkā: 'will it follow you to break up the valley soil?' — the plowing-service that requires an obedient, domesticated animal. The wild ox cannot be directed to human agricultural ends.
11: Hătiḇṭaḥ bô kî-rab kōḥô: 'Will you trust it because its strength is great?' — the irony: the wild ox has enormous power (kōaḥ) but none of it is usable for human purposes. Wĕʿāzabtā ʾēlāyw yĕḡîʿekā: 'will you leave your heavy work to it?' — yĕḡîʿ (labor, toil): the hardest agricultural tasks.
12: Hătaʾămîn bô kî-yāšûḇ zarʿekā: 'Will you count on it to bring in your harvest?' — ʾāman (to trust): the trust that domesticated animals deserve but the wild ox does not. Wĕgārnĕkā yĕʾassōp: 'and gather it to your threshing floor?' — the threshing floor as the culmination of agricultural effort: the wild ox cannot be trusted to complete the cycle.
13: Kenap rĕnānîm neʿĕlāsāh: 'Is the wing of the ostrich a joyful flapping?' — the ostrich-verse is textually difficult. Rĕnānîm (ostriches, or cries of joy): a plural used for ostriches from their cry. ʾIm ʾeḇrāh ḥăsîḏāh wĕnōṣāh: 'or the feathers of the stork?' — ḥăsîḏāh (stork/the loyal bird, from ḥeseḏ = steadfast love): the irony that the loyal-bird metaphor is here applied to the ostrich's apparently disloyal abandonment of eggs. MT text is uncertain.
14: Kî-taʿăzōḇ lāʾāreṣ bêṣêhā: 'She abandons her eggs in the dirt' — ʿāzaḇ (to abandon, forsake): the same root used for divine abandonment in lament psalms. The ostrich's egg-abandonment is contrasted with the mountain goat's careful birth attendance (v1). Both are God's design.
15: Wattiškaḥ kî-regel tĕzûrāh: 'Forgetting that a foot might trample them' — šāḵaḥ (to forget): the ostrich is characterized by forgetting. The apparent cruelty of the design — eggs left exposed to trampling — is the point: God made a creature that operates entirely outside the framework of protective care that humans value.
16: Hiqšîḥāh libânêhā lōʾ lāh: 'She treats her chicks as if they are not hers' — qāšāh (to harden, make callous): the hardened indifference. Rîq yĕḡîʿāh bĕlî-paḥaḏ: 'her labor is for nothing, without anxiety' — no maternal anxiety, no concern for the investment. The ostrich's behavior is not cruel by design; it is outside the categories of care and cruelty.
17: Kî hišʾāh ʾĕlôah ḥāḵmāh: 'For God has withheld wisdom from her' — šāʾāh (to withhold, neglect): the explicit divine explanation: God gave the ostrich no wisdom in this domain. Wĕlōʾ-ḥālaq lāh babbînāh: 'and given her no share of understanding' — the Creator distributed wisdom unevenly by design. Wisdom is not universal; its presence and absence in creatures is God's prerogative.
18: Kāʿēt tāmĕrîa harōmmāh tśaḥaq lassûs: 'When she raises herself to run, she laughs at the horse' — the compensating gift: the ostrich that cannot fly and abandons her eggs runs faster than any horse. ûlĕrōkĕḇô: 'and its rider' — the creature without wisdom in one domain possesses extraordinary power in another. Each creature in God's gallery has its own specific excellence.
19: Hătittēn lassûs gĕḇûrāh: 'Did you give the horse its might?' — gĕḇûrāh (might, strength, power): the same term used of divine power in psalms (Psalms 21:14; Job 29:1). The war-horse is God's creature demonstrating divine power aesthetically. Hălbiššô raʿmāh ṣawwāʾrô: 'did you clothe its neck with a mane/thunder?' — raʿmāh (thunder, or mane — the term is disputed): the neck of the war-horse is clothed with what shakes like thunder.
20: Hatarzeʾennû kāʾarḇeh: 'Can you make it spring/leap like a locust?' — rāzaʿ (to cause to leap, shudder): the locust-comparison for the horse's spring. Hôḏ naḥrô ʾêmāh: 'the majesty of its snorting is terrifying' — hôḏ (majesty, splendor): the divine-attribute term applied to the horse's snort. The horse embodies in animal form the attributes of divine power.
21: Yaḥpĕrû bāʾēmeq wayyāśîś bĕḵōaḥ: 'It paws the valley and surges forward in strength' — ḥāpar (to dig, paw the ground): the pre-charge preparation. Yēṣēʾ liqraʾt-nāšeq: 'it charges into the battle' — the battle-vocabulary: the horse is a purpose-built creation for warfare, going out (yāṣāʾ) to meet armed conflict.
22: Yiśḥaq lĕpaḥaḏ wĕlōʾ yēḥāt: 'It laughs at fear and is not alarmed' — śāḥaq (to laugh): the horse's fearlessness expressed as laughter at what frightens others. Wĕlōʾ-yāšûḇ mipnê-ḥāreb: 'it does not turn back from the sword' — the charge that does not retreat. Courage as a designed animal characteristic.
23: ʿālāyw tirtaqqeš ʾašpāh: 'The quiver rattles on its flank' — the horse carries the archer's equipment. Lahab ḥănît ûkîḏôn: 'the flashing spear and javelin' — lahab (flame, flash): the weapons' glint in battle. The full military array.
24: Bĕrāgaz wĕraʿaš yĕgammaʾ-ʾāreṣ: 'With fierce excitement it devours the ground' — rāgaz (to tremble, be excited): the horse's physical agitation before battle. Wĕlōʾ-yaʾămin kî-qôl šôpār: 'at the sound of the trumpet it cannot stand still' — the trumpet (šôpār) triggers uncontrollable movement. The horse is designed for battle as surely as the eagle for soaring.
25: Bĕdê šôpār yōmar heʾāḥ: 'At the trumpet blast it cries Aha!' — the horse's battle-cry, heʾāḥ: the triumph sound. Yārîaḥ milḥāmāh mērāḥôq: 'it scents the battle from far away' — the combat-sense as a designed organ. Raʿam śārîm ûtĕrûʿāh: 'the thunder of commanders and the battle cry' — the horse responds to all of these with increasing excitement, not retreat.
26: Hămibbiynāṯĕkā yaʾbēr nēṣ: 'Does the hawk soar by your wisdom?' — nēṣ (hawk, falcon): the migratory raptor. Yiprōś kĕnāpāyw lĕtêmān: 'does it spread its wings toward the south?' — the migratory instinct, the seasonal southward flight: no human programmed this. The hawk's migration is God's design, not human wisdom.
27: ʾIm-ʿal-pîkā yiḡbah nāšer: 'Does the eagle soar at your command?' — nāšer (eagle, griffon vulture): the great soaring raptor. Wĕḵî yārîm qinnô: 'does it build its nest on the heights?' — the nest-placement on inaccessible heights: not human instruction but divine endowment.
28: Sālaʿ yiškon wĕyitlônān: 'It lives and stays on the cliff' — sālaʿ (cliff, crag): the eagle's chosen dwelling. ʿal-šen-seleʿ ûmĕṣûḏāh: 'on the rocky crag and the natural stronghold' — mĕṣûḏāh (fortress, stronghold): God placed the eagle's home in the equivalent of a natural fortification.
29: Miššam yāpîr-ʾōḵel: 'From there it watches for food' — ṣāpar (to look out): the eagle's survey-vision from its height-advantage. Lĕmērāḥôq ʿênāyw yabbîṭû: 'its eyes spot prey from far away' — the eagle's visual acuity as a God-designed tool. The animal gallery ends with a creature that exemplifies both transcendence (height) and precision (sight).
30: ûpĕrāḥāyw yĕʿalĕṭû-ḏām: 'Its young ones drink blood' — the eagle chick nourished on prey-blood. ûbăʾăšer ḥălālîm šām hûʾ: 'where the slain are, there the eagle is' — quoted in Matthew 24:28 / Luke 17:37 by Jesus: 'where the corpse is, there the eagles will gather.' The eagle as nature's end-of-life functionary, designed to recycle the dead. The divine provision for death is part of the created order.
